profiles.bio: I'm drawn to spaces where people show up fully — where there's room for both the practical and the profound. I've been practicing in intentional community contexts for about eight years, mostly through somatic work and systems thinking. These days I'm trying to understand what it means to be genuinely helpful without losing myself in the process.
profiles.currentIntention: A world where depth is normal — where the quality of attention we bring each other is considered as important as output.

profiles.bio: I'm a researcher and facilitator interested in how groups develop trust over time. I spent several years living in an ecovillage in Wales before moving to Berlin, where I now work with organisations navigating complexity. I find that the quality of attention we bring to each other is the most underrated resource in any group.
profiles.currentIntention: Groups that know how to slow down together when it matters.

profiles.bio: I run community learning programs in Nairobi focused on young people and participatory futures. I came to IS through a curiosity about how small, intentional groups can hold the kind of depth that most institutions can't. I'm interested in what changes when people feel genuinely seen.
profiles.currentIntention: Learning communities that don't extract from the people inside them.

profiles.bio: I work as a therapist with a background in phenomenology, and I'm genuinely curious about the line between personal development and relational practice. I came to IS through Marcus after years of feeling that most professional development spaces were missing something I couldn't name. I'm still trying to name it.

I'm a software engineer who spends too much time thinking about attention, distraction, and what we optimise for. I've been building a personal contemplative practice for about three years, mostly through Vipassana retreats and a lot of journaling. I joined IS because I wanted to practise in community, not just alone.
profiles.currentIntention: A life where stillness and engagement aren't in conflict.

profiles.bio: I live and work in a co-housing community in Copenhagen and have been involved in dialogue practices for about a decade. I'm particularly interested in the design of containers — what makes a space safe enough for real things to be said. I also make furniture, which is a different kind of attention entirely.
profiles.supplementaryInfo: Currently facilitating a neighbourhood dialogue process around shared green space.

profiles.bio: I'm a writer and educator interested in the intersection of narrative, memory, and belonging. I grew up between cultures and have spent a lot of my adult life trying to understand how people make sense of where they come from. IS feels like a space where that kind of inquiry is welcome.
profiles.currentIntention: Stories that help people locate themselves.

profiles.bio: I practise Zen and work as a landscape architect, two disciplines that turn out to share more than I expected. I'm interested in IS as a place to explore depth with people who are serious about it. I prefer slow conversation to fast networking.

I'm a public health researcher with a long-standing interest in how communities heal collectively, not just individually. I've been involved in women's circles for many years and found that the principles transfer widely. I'm here to think with others about scale, care, and what we owe each other.
profiles.currentIntention: Health systems that treat communities as the unit of care.

profiles.bio: I'm a mediator and conflict resolution practitioner, which means I spend most of my professional life in rooms where people can't hear each other. IS is where I come to remember what good listening feels like. I'm also a distance runner, which is its own kind of practice.

I work in arts administration and have spent years trying to create conditions for creative risk-taking in institutional settings — which turns out to be mostly relational work. I was drawn to IS through an interest in how groups hold space for vulnerability without making it a performance.
profiles.currentIntention: Cultural institutions that are genuinely changed by the artists inside them.

profiles.bio: I'm a philosopher by training and a translator by trade, which means I think a lot about the gap between what we mean and what we say. I found IS through a search for rigorous but warm thinking communities — places that take ideas seriously without losing sight of the people having them.

I run a small social enterprise and have a long interest in cooperative structures and participatory decision-making. I'm sceptical of most community spaces but keep coming back to IS because the quality of presence here is different. I'm trying to understand why.
profiles.currentIntention: Organisations that don't reproduce the hierarchies they set out to change.

profiles.bio: I'm a nurse and a longtime practitioner of non-violent communication. I came to IS through a friend who said it was the only online space where she felt less lonely rather than more. I'm interested in what makes that possible and how to bring some of it into clinical settings.

I study organisational psychology and am curious about what conditions allow people to take interpersonal risks. I've been in and out of several community spaces over the years and find that IS holds something different — a willingness to sit with not knowing. I'm still figuring out what to do with that.
profiles.currentIntention: Research that actually changes the conditions it studies.

profiles.bio: I'm an architect and urban researcher focused on how built environments shape or constrain the possibility of genuine encounter. After years of designing spaces, I became more interested in what actually happens inside them. IS felt like a community that was asking the same question from a different angle.
profiles.currentIntention: Cities designed around the quality of attention people can bring each other.

profiles.bio: I work in child welfare policy and have spent fifteen years watching systems fail the people they were designed to help. I came to IS looking for a different kind of conversation — one that holds both the systemic and the personal without collapsing one into the other. Still finding my footing here.

I'm a musician and sound practitioner interested in how listening shapes community. I've been facilitating sound meditation sessions for small groups in Lagos for the past four years, and I'm curious about what happens when you take the same quality of presence into conversation. IS is part of that experiment.
profiles.currentIntention: Communities that listen as well as they speak.

profiles.bio: I'm a somatic therapist and former ballet dancer. My work is about helping people reconnect with what their bodies already know — which turns out to overlap a lot with what good community practice is trying to do. I joined IS after years of searching for a space that didn't treat depth as a liability.
profiles.currentIntention: A culture where embodied intelligence is taken as seriously as verbal intelligence.

profiles.bio: I organize mutual-aid networks and design rituals that help diaspora communities stay woven together across distance. My work lives where the logistical and the sacred meet.
profiles.currentIntention: Communities where belonging doesn't depend on proximity.

profiles.bio: Cooperative-economy nerd and amateur cellist. I'm interested in how groups make decisions together without grinding themselves down.
Somatic therapist and conflict mediator. I keep coming back to the question of how bodies hold what words can't yet say.
profiles.currentIntention: A culture fluent enough in repair that conflict stops being frightening.

profiles.bio: Permaculturist working on urban food systems. Most of what I know about community I learned from tending soil.
Designer with a long contemplative practice. I try to make tools that leave people calmer than they found them.
profiles.currentIntention: Technology that returns attention rather than mining it.

profiles.bio: I run a youth theater collective and tinker with civic tech on the side. Storytelling and infrastructure are the same craft to me.
Peacebuilder and poet. I've spent years in rooms where people who were taught to fear each other slowly learn to share a meal.
profiles.currentIntention: More tables where former enemies can eat in peace.

profiles.bio: Apprenticed in traditional craft and tea. I'm drawn to communities that treat patience as a skill worth teaching.
Ecologist who stumbled into grief work. I help groups metabolize loss so it composts into something that can grow.
profiles.currentIntention: Ways of grieving together that the modern world forgot.

profiles.bio: Community organizer and documentary maker. I document the quiet resilience that doesn't make the news.
I facilitate parenting circles grounded in nonviolent communication. Raising kids turned out to be the most radical community work I've done.
profiles.currentIntention: Households where children grow up assuming repair is normal.

profiles.bio: Jazz musician and educator. I think the best classrooms work like a good band — structured enough to improvise.
Dancer-turned-researcher studying how movement shapes group trust. My lab is mostly other people's living rooms.
profiles.currentIntention: Research that takes the body as seriously as the mind.

profiles.bio: Open-source maintainer fascinated by how online commons govern themselves. The healthy projects feel a lot like healthy neighborhoods.
Long-time meditator working on the ethics of biotech. I sit with hard questions for a living, literally and figuratively.
profiles.currentIntention: Powerful tools held by people who've done their inner work.

profiles.bio: Founder who got tired of building things that left people lonelier. Now I host men's circles and think about regenerative business.
Dialogue facilitator and trauma-informed musician. I use song to get people into the same room before the hard conversation starts.
profiles.currentIntention: Songs that can hold a room together through a hard truth.

profiles.bio: Muralist and popular educator. I believe a wall painted together is worth ten meetings.
Public-health worker who runs women's circles in the evenings. Health, I've learned, is mostly a function of who you can call at 2am.
profiles.currentIntention: Care systems that count belonging as a vital sign.

profiles.bio: Architect specializing in cohousing. I design buildings that make it slightly awkward to avoid your neighbors — on purpose.
Philosopher and facilitator working on collective futures. I help groups remember they're allowed to want something better.
profiles.currentIntention: Groups bold enough to imagine past the next quarter.

programs.name: The Gumball Machine
programs.description: (LOCAL DEV) For those who want to meet new people but aren't sure where to start, pull a lever and get matched with a stranger for an intro call, and be surprised by who you meet.

programs.name: Presence Pods (2026 Q2)
programs.description: (LOCAL DEV) For those craving real connection and wanting to feel truly seen, join a small group of four rotating over four calls where each person gets a turn being held by the group's warm, Circling-style curiosity and reflection. Running Q2 of 2026.

programs.name: Community Calls
programs.description: (LOCAL DEV) Running for 5+ years as the heartbeat of mutual support in this ecosystem, community members gather each Sunday for developmental-relational practices and growth-edge explorations. Growth, belonging, companionship, solidarity, all rooted in "being who we want to be".

programs.name: Casework Pods (2026 Q3)
programs.description: (LOCAL DEV) For those who want support with something real and to offer that same support to others, bring a live challenge into a group of four rotating over four calls where each person gets a turn being met with empathic reflection focused on what they're navigating. Running Q3 of 2026.

programs.name: Arts in IS
programs.description: (LOCAL DEV) This group believes artistic expression has the capacity to be a vibrant part of culture and personal development. We meet as a group to encourage exploration of multiple modes of artistic creation, develop ways to do co-creation, and offer sessions at occasional IS community calls.

programs.name: Weekly Web Updates
programs.description: (LOCAL DEV) This is a subscription to the weekly internal newsletter containing event links and announcements across the IS Web.

invites.note: Marcus brings rare depth as a researcher and facilitator. He's thought carefully about trust in groups for years — exactly what this community is built around.
Leila has been building learning communities in Nairobi with a quality of attention I don't often encounter. She'll bring a grounding perspective we don't yet have.
Tobias and I met at a conference on phenomenology and community. He asks the kind of questions that slow a room down in the best possible way.
Priya has been building a serious contemplative practice alongside her work as an engineer. She's thoughtful, honest about her uncertainties, and ready for this.
Søren is part of a co-housing community in Copenhagen doing adjacent work to ours. His perspective on container design would resonate here immediately.
